title: What Am I Tracking That Nobody Else Is?
subtitle: A journal practice for making the invisible mental load visible
excerpt: A journal practice for making explicit the invisible tasks, concerns, and tracking work that you are doing that nobody else in your household or workplace is doing. For the feeling of being the only one who knows what needs to happen next.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. Draws on the concept of the 'mental load' as developed in gender studies and popularised by illustrator Emma (You Should Have Asked, 2017).

content.text: A journal practice for making the mental load visible. The mental load is the ongoing tracking and planning work that keeps a household or a team functioning: knowing when appointments need booking, when things are running out, when someone needs to be somewhere, what has been agreed and with whom. This practice puts it on the page.
List everything you are currently holding in your head that relates to other people's needs, schedules, or wellbeing.
Everything. Doctor appointments, school events, someone's food preferences, a work deadline that affects your team. The whole list, even if it takes fifteen minutes.
Of the items on that list, which ones is nobody else tracking?
Circle or mark them. These are the ones that fall entirely to you. Write how many there are.
Which of these could be handed to someone else, if you asked?
Not whether you think they will do it as well as you. Whether it is possible in principle for someone else to hold it.
What would you need to give up control of in order to hand it over?
Write what the handover would actually need. Telling them, accepting a different standard, putting up with some mess. Name the real obstacle.
Written for homemade.education. The mental load idea was made popular by French illustrator Emma in 2017. It builds on earlier feminist writing about the unseen work of running a home.
